summary: The DataSource + Settings Bridge is well-structured and follows OBP patterns correctly. All 5 OBP rules are satisfied: behavior lives on the owning object (SqliteDataSource owns CRUD, SettingsData owns navigation), handlers navigate the object graph (Context.Engine.System.DataSource), object references are preserved (Actor stores Engine, SettingsData stores _engine), per-request state is passed through parameters, and no collection iteration violations exist. Error handling is comprehensive at the SqliteDataSource level (SqliteException + IOException + JsonException). The LazyParamsGenerator's resolution error propagation is correctly implemented — errors short-circuit before Run(). Two minor gaps exist in exception filter completeness (DeserializeValue and EnsureTable performance), plus two nits. No critical or major findings. Tests are thorough: 22 DataSource tests, 18 SettingsData tests including corrupt-DB error path, 4 new MemoryStack navigation tests. All 1465 C# tests pass.

findings.issue: DeserializeValue catches only JsonException but calls Data.UnwrapJsonElement which throws InvalidOperationException at depth > 128. The outer Get() catch filter (SqliteException | IOException) does not cover InvalidOperationException either, so it propagates uncaught through SettingsData.GetChild to the caller.
findings.impact: If a user stores externally-sourced deeply nested JSON (>128 levels after unwrapping) as a settings value, the unhandled exception propagates to the step runner. Realistic exploitation requires unusual conditions — JsonDocument.Parse default MaxDepth=64 catches most cases first. The gap is narrow but violates the 'behavior methods never throw' contract on IDataSource.
findings.suggestion: Add catch (InvalidOperationException) alongside catch (JsonException) in DeserializeValue, returning the raw JSON string as fallback (same as the JsonException path). This is a one-line addition.

findings.issue: EnsureTable opens a new connection and runs CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS on every CRUD call (Get, Set, Remove, Exists, GetAll). For hot paths like %Settings.X% resolved on every step in a loop, this is redundant I/O — the table was already created on the first access.
findings.impact: Performance degradation under high-frequency settings access. SQLite connection pooling mitigates connection overhead but the DDL statement still executes and acquires a schema lock each time. Not a correctness issue.
findings.suggestion: Cache known table names in a HashSet<string> field. After EnsureTable succeeds for a table, add it to the set and skip subsequent calls. Clear on Dispose.

findings.issue: The _disposed field is set in Dispose() but never checked in public methods (Get/Set/Remove/Exists/GetAll/Tables). Operations after disposal produce confusing SqliteException rather than clear ObjectDisposedException.
findings.impact: Misleading error classification during shutdown races. The SqliteException IS caught by existing catch blocks, so no crash — just wrong error key. Low priority since disposal only happens at engine shutdown.
findings.suggestion: Add a ThrowIfDisposed() guard at the top of each public method, or accept as known limitation and document it.

findings.issue: SettingsData.GetChild creates a new Data with `child.Context = Context` (line 66), but Context on SettingsData may be null if it hasn't been propagated yet. This is safe — Data.Context setter handles null — but the explicit assignment is redundant when Context is null.
findings.impact: None. Cosmetic only — the null propagation is handled correctly by the Data.Context setter.
findings.suggestion: No change needed. The explicit assignment is defensive and harmless.
